choices.prompt: According to the module, what does an Azure Cosmos DB container hold?
choices: Items — schemaless JSON documents
Tables with a fixed relational schema
Blobs of binary data with a pointer index
Stored procedures that define the partition key

choices.prompt: Meridian's nightly bulk import throws HTTP 429 errors even though the account has plenty of total RU/s capacity. Based on the module, what is the most likely cause?
choices: The import fires thousands of writes into one logical partition faster than that partition's share of RU/s allows
The account default consistency level was set to strong, which rejects bulk writes
The items exceed the maximum item size, so Cosmos rejects them as 429s
DefaultAzureCredential expired mid-import and the writes were unauthorized
correct_answers: The import fires thousands of writes into one logical partition faster than that partition's share of RU/s allows

choices.prompt: In the walkthrough, what must be supplied to perform the cheapest possible point read with container.read_item?
choices: Both the item id and the partition key value
Only the item id, since Cosmos infers the partition
Only the partition key value, which uniquely identifies the item
A SQL query string filtering on the partition key
correct_answers: Both the item id and the partition key value

choices.prompt: Which statements about consistency levels does the module make? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Session is the default and guarantees that within a single client session you read your own writes
Strong guarantees a read always sees the latest committed write but costs more RUs and adds latency
Eventual is the most expensive level but offers the strongest guarantee across regions
Cosmos offers five consistency levels along a spectrum from strong to eventual
correct_answers: Session is the default and guarantees that within a single client session you read your own writes
Strong guarantees a read always sees the latest committed write but costs more RUs and adds latency
Cosmos offers five consistency levels along a spectrum from strong to eventual

choices.prompt: Which of the following are listed as common pitfalls in the module? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Choosing a low-cardinality or time-based partition key, which creates hot partitions and 429s
Querying without the partition key, which forces an expensive cross-partition fan-out query
Storing huge items instead of putting large blobs in Blob Storage with a pointer in Cosmos
Provisioning throughput with autoscale, which the module warns prevents the SDK from retrying 429s
correct_answers: Choosing a low-cardinality or time-based partition key, which creates hot partitions and 429s
Querying without the partition key, which forces an expensive cross-partition fan-out query
Storing huge items instead of putting large blobs in Blob Storage with a pointer in Cosmos

llm.prompt: A container partitioned by /orderDate throttles with 429s every night at midnight even though total provisioned RU/s is barely used. Explain why this happens and how you would fix it.
llm.reference_answer: Throughput in Cosmos DB is provisioned and enforced per physical partition, not just per account, so a time-based key like /orderDate funnels every write at a given moment onto a single logical partition value. That creates a hot partition that exhausts its own share of RU/s while the other partitions sit idle, producing 429 Too Many Requests even though account-wide capacity looks fine. The durable fix is to repartition on a high-cardinality key aligned with the common query filter (for example /customerId) so writes spread evenly across partitions. You can also pace the import, spread writes across partition-key values, or raise throughput with autoscale to absorb the midnight spike.

llm.prompt: Compare session consistency and eventual consistency, and explain which one resolves Meridian's dashboard showing a delivery as both 'in transit' and 'delivered' at the lowest cost.
llm.reference_answer: Eventual consistency is the cheapest and lowest-latency level, but a read may return an older value for a while because a replica hasn't caught up yet — exactly what caused the dashboard glitch when it read across two regions during a write. Session consistency, the account default, guarantees read-your-own-writes within a single client session, giving the monotonic behavior the workload actually assumed it had. Choosing session (or bounded staleness with a defined lag) fixes the stale-read problem at much lower RU cost than strong consistency, which would add extra RUs and cross-region latency. Since the requirement is just to stop seeing stale values, session is the cheapest level that resolves it.

llm.prompt: Walk through how you would create a delivery item, read it back cheaply, and update its status using the azure-cosmos Python SDK, and explain how you would measure the real RU cost of the update.
llm.reference_answer: First create the client with CosmosClient using the endpoint and DefaultAzureCredential so no keys live in code, then create the database and a 'deliveries' container with create_container_if_not_exists, setting partition_key to PartitionKey(path='/customerId') and offer_throughput to 400 (the manual-throughput minimum). Create the delivery with container.create_item(body=delivery), ensuring the partition-key property (customerId) is present on the document. Read it back with container.read_item(item='dlv-90187', partition_key='cust-4521'); supplying both the id and the partition key makes this a single-partition point read costing about 1 RU instead of a fan-out query. To update, set item['status'] = 'delivered' and call container.replace_item, then read container.client_connection.last_response_headers['x-ms-request-charge'] to measure the real RU cost rather than guessing.
